question: The rate of a chemical reaction doubles for every $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ rise of temperature. If the temperature is raised by $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the rate of the reaction increases by about :

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following data for the given reaction
2HI(g)→H2( g)+I2( g)

HImolL-1                     0.005         0.01         0.02

Rate molL-1 s-1      7.5×10-4      3.0×10-3       1.2×10-2

The order of the reaction is __________.
question:
Consider the following data for the given reaction
2HI(g)→H2( g)+I2( g)

HImolL-1                     0.005         0.01         0.02

Rate molL-1 s-1      7.5×10-4      3.0×10-3       1.2×10-2

The order of the reaction is __________.
solution:
The initial concentrations of HI as 0.005, 0.01, and 0.02 mol/L, respectively, and the corresponding initial rates as given:

HImolL-1                     0.005         0.01         0.02

Rate molL-1 s-1      7.5×10-4      3.0×10-3       1.2×10-2

 For the reaction, rate law is
rate=kHIP
P is the order of the reaction.
From the given data
7.5×10-4=k0.05P-------(1)
3×10-3=k0.01P  --------(2)
1.2×10-2=k0.02P ---------(3)
On dividing Equation (3) by equation (2)
12×10-33×10-3=k0.02Pk0.01P
⇒2P=22
⇒P=2

subject: Chemistry
q:
At $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in presence of a catalyst, activation energy of a reaction is lowered by $10 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The logarithm of ratio of $\frac{\mathrm{k} \text { (catalysed) }}{\mathrm{k} \text { (uncatalysed) }}$ is....
(Consider that the frequency factor for both the reactions is same)
question:
At $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in presence of a catalyst, activation energy of a reaction is lowered by $10 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The logarithm of ratio of $\frac{\mathrm{k} \text { (catalysed) }}{\mathrm{k} \text { (uncatalysed) }}$ is....
(Consider that the frequency factor for both the reactions is same)
solution:
Using the Arrhenius equation: $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$
The ratio of rate constants is: $\frac{k_{catalysed}}{k_{uncatalysed}} = \frac{A e^{-E_a^c/RT}}{A e^{-E_a^u/RT}} = e^{(E_a^u - E_a^c)/RT} = e^{\Delta E_a/RT}$
Since the catalyst lowers activation energy by 10 kJ/mol: $\Delta E_a = 10$ kJ/mol = 10000 J/mol
At T = 27°C = 300 K, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K):
$\frac{k_c}{k_u} = e^{10000/(8.314 \times 300)} = e^{10000/2494.2} = e^{4.009}$
Taking logarithm base 10: $\log_{10}\frac{k_c}{k_u} = 4.009 \times \log_{10}(e) = 4.009 \times 0.4343 = 1.741$

q: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>518</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the rate of decomposition of a sample of gaseous acetaldehyde, initially at a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>363</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>Torr</mi></math> was <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.00</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>Torr</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math> had reacted and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.50</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>Torr</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> when 33% had reacted. subject: Chemistry
q: If 50 % of a reaction occurs in 100 second and 75 % of the reaction occurs in 200 second, the order of this reaction is:
question: If 50 % of a reaction occurs in 100 second and 75 % of the reaction occurs in 200 second, the order of this reaction is:
solution:
50% of a reaction occurs in 100 second and 75% of the reaction occurs in 200 second, the order of this reaction is 1.

Let the initial reactant concentration is 1 M.

In the first 100 seconds, reactant concentration is reduced to 50% or 1×50100​=0.5 M.

In the second 100 seconds (total 200 seconds from start), reactant concentration is reduced to 100−75=25% or  1× 25100​ =0.25 M..

Thus, we observe that after each 100 second period, the reactant concentration is reduced to one half. Hence, 100 seconds is the half-life period and is independent of reactant concentration. This is characteristic of the first-order reaction.



First-order reaction as the half-life is constant.

q:
The rate of a reaction quadruples when the temperature changes from 300  to 310 K . The activation energy of this reaction is:

(Assume Activation energy and pre-exponential factor are independent of temperature; ln(2)=0.693; R=8.314 J mol-1K-1)
question:
The rate of a reaction quadruples when the temperature changes from 300  to 310 K . The activation energy of this reaction is:

(Assume Activation energy and pre-exponential factor are independent of temperature; ln(2)=0.693; R=8.314 J mol-1K-1)
solution:
The activation energy, rate constant and temperature can be related using Arrhenius equation.

k=Ae-EaRT

lnk2k1=EaR 1T1-1T2 

ln4=EaR 1300-1310 

2×0.693=EaR 10300×310 

E a  =  2×0.693×300×310×8.314 10  = 107.2 kJ/mol

subject: Chemistry
q:
The reaction $2 \mathrm{X} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ is a zeroth order reaction. If the

initial concentration of $\mathrm{X}$ is $0.2 \mathrm{M}$, the half-life is $6 \mathrm{~h}$. When the initial concentration of $X$ is $0.5 \mathrm{M}$, the time required to reach its final concentration of $0.2 \mathrm{M}$ will be
question:
The reaction $2 \mathrm{X} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ is a zeroth order reaction. If the

initial concentration of $\mathrm{X}$ is $0.2 \mathrm{M}$, the half-life is $6 \mathrm{~h}$. When the initial concentration of $X$ is $0.5 \mathrm{M}$, the time required to reach its final concentration of $0.2 \mathrm{M}$ will be
solution:
For the reaction $2 \mathrm{X} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$, follow zeroth order Rate equation is $\mathrm{Kt}=[\mathrm{A}]_{0}-[\mathrm{A}]$

For the half-life; $t=t_{1 / 2}$ and $[A]=0.1$ $\mathrm{Kt}_{12}=0.2-0.1$

$\frac{0.2-0.1}{6}=\frac{0.1}{6} \mathrm{Mhr}^{-1}$

$\therefore$ Time required to reach from $0.5 \mathrm{M}$ to $0.2 \mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{Kt}=[\mathrm{A}]_{0}-[\mathrm{A}]$

$\frac{0.1}{6} \times \mathrm{t}=(0.5-0.2) ; \mathrm{t}=18$ hour

Once it reaches there, the bacterial population goes down as <math><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> . What will be the plot of <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> vs <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> after <math><mn>1</mn></math> hour?
question: A bacterial infection in an internal wound grows as <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>)</mo></math> , where the time <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> is in hours. A dose of antibiotic, taken orally, needs 1 hour to reach the wound. Once it reaches there, the bacterial population goes down as <math><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> . What will be the plot of <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> vs <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> after <math><mn>1</mn></math> hour?
solution:
From 0 to 1 hour, N'=N0et
From 1 hour onwards dNdt=-5N2
So at t=1 hour, N'=eN0
dNdt=-5N2
∫eN0NN-2dN=-5∫1tdt
1N-1eN0=5t-1
N0N-1e=5N0(t-1)
N0N=5N0(t-1)+1e
N0N=5N0t+1e-5N0
Which is following y=mx+C

subject: Chemistry
q:
Drug $X$ becomes ineffective after $50 \%$ decomposition. The original concentration of drug in a bottle was $16 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{mL}$ which becomes $4 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{mL}$ in 12 months. The expiry time of the drug in months is __________$\qquad$
Assume that the decomposition of the drug follows first order kinetics.
question:
Drug $X$ becomes ineffective after $50 \%$ decomposition. The original concentration of drug in a bottle was $16 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{mL}$ which becomes $4 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{mL}$ in 12 months. The expiry time of the drug in months is __________$\qquad$
Assume that the decomposition of the drug follows first order kinetics.
solution:
Drug $\mathrm{X} \xrightarrow{\text { 1st order }}$ Product
Initial concentration of drug $=16 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{mL}$
Concentration of drug after 12 months $=4 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{mL}$
Half life of drug = 6 months
Drug become ineffective after 50\% decomposition. The expiry time of drug $=6$ months

subject: Chemistry
q:
If the half life of a first order reaction is $6.93$ minutes then the time required for completion of $99$% of the reaction will be _____ minutes.
(Given : $\log 2 = 0.3010$)
question:
If the half life of a first order reaction is $6.93$ minutes then the time required for completion of $99$% of the reaction will be _____ minutes.
(Given : $\log 2 = 0.3010$)
solution:
For a first-order reaction, the rate constant $k$ is given by:

$k = \dfrac{2.303 \log 2}{t_{1/2}}$

Using the standard approximation $2.303 \log 2 \approx 0.693$:

$k = \dfrac{0.693}{6.93} = 0.1 \text{ min}^{-1}$

The time required for $99\%$ completion of the reaction is given by the integrated rate law:

$t = \dfrac{2.303}{k} \log \left( \dfrac{a}{a - x} \right)$

Here, the initial concentration $a = 100$ and the amount reacted $x = 99$, so $a - x = 1$.

$t = \dfrac{2.303}{0.1} \log \left( \dfrac{100}{1} \right)$

$t = 23.03 \times \log(10^2)$

$t = 23.03 \times 2 = 46.06 \text{ minutes}$

Answer: $46.06$

subject: Chemistry
q:
A→BReactantProduct

If formation of compound B follows the first order of kinetics and after 70 minutes the concentration of A was found to be half of its initial concentration. Then the rate constant of the reaction is x×10-6 s-1. The value of x is____
question:
A→BReactantProduct

If formation of compound B follows the first order of kinetics and after 70 minutes the concentration of A was found to be half of its initial concentration. Then the rate constant of the reaction is x×10-6 s-1. The value of x is____
solution:
A→BReactantProduct

Since the reaction follows the first order kinetics and after 70 minutes the concentration of A was found to be half of its initial concentration. That means 70 minutes is the half life time for the reaction. And we know that for a first order reaction half life time is given as.

t12 = 0.693KK=0.693t12=0.69370×60 sec.

=69307×6×10-6 sec-1= 165 ×10-6 sec-1So, x = 165

subject: Chemistry
q:
A person's wound was exposed to some bacteria and then bacteria growth started to happen at the same place. The wound was later treated with some antibacterial medicine and the rate of bacterial decay (r) was found to be proportional with the square of the existing number of bacteria at any instance. Which of the following set of graphs correctly represents the 'before' and 'after' situation of the application of the medicine?
[Given : $\mathrm{N}=$ No. of bacteria, $\mathrm{t}=$ time, bacterial growth follows $I^{\text {st }}$ order kinetics.]
question:
A person's wound was exposed to some bacteria and then bacteria growth started to happen at the same place. The wound was later treated with some antibacterial medicine and the rate of bacterial decay (r) was found to be proportional with the square of the existing number of bacteria at any instance. Which of the following set of graphs correctly represents the 'before' and 'after' situation of the application of the medicine?
[Given : $\mathrm{N}=$ No. of bacteria, $\mathrm{t}=$ time, bacterial growth follows $I^{\text {st }}$ order kinetics.]
solution:
*Before applying medicine
$\frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{K}[\mathrm{A}]$ (First order growth) (Rate law)
$\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{~A}_0}=\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{~N}_0}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{Kt}}$
*After applying medicine

$\mathrm{r}=-\frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{K}[\mathrm{A}]^2 \quad$ (Rate law)
$y=\mathrm{Kx}^2$ Parabola

subject: Chemistry
q: For a reversible reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>⇌</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> forward reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The activation energy of the uncatalyzed forward reaction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. When the reaction is catalysed keeping the reactant concentration same, the rate of the catalysed forward reaction at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>27</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is found to be same as that of the uncatalyzed reaction at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>327</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The activation energy of the catalysed backward reaction is _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: For a reversible reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>⇌</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> forward reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The activation energy of the uncatalyzed forward reaction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. When the reaction is catalysed keeping the reactant concentration same, the rate of the catalysed forward reaction at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>27</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is found to be same as that of the uncatalyzed reaction at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>327</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The activation energy of the catalysed backward reaction is _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
As per the question 

To determine the activation energy of the catalyzed backward reaction, we need to use the Arrhenius equation and the given information.

The Arrhenius equation is given by: 

k=Ae(-Ea/RT)

Ae-300×103600×R=Ae-Ea300×R

⇒1032=Ea300

⇒Ea=150×103J mol–1

⇒Ea=150 kJ mol–1

∴ Activation energy of catalysed backward reaction

Energy of activation for backward reactionEb=Ea-∆H

=150-20=130 kJ mol–1

subject: Chemistry
q: Decomposition of A is a first order reaction at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$ and is given by $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{B}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})$. In a closed 1 L vessel, 1 bar $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g})$ is allowed to decompose at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$. After 100 minutes, the total pressure was 1.5 bar. What is the rate constant $\left(\mathrm{in} \mathrm{min}^{-1}\right)$ of the reaction ? $(\log 2=0.3)$
question: Decomposition of A is a first order reaction at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$ and is given by $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{B}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})$. In a closed 1 L vessel, 1 bar $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g})$ is allowed to decompose at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$. After 100 minutes, the total pressure was 1.5 bar. What is the rate constant $\left(\mathrm{in} \mathrm{min}^{-1}\right)$ of the reaction ? $(\log 2=0.3)$
solution:
For the reaction $A(g) \rightarrow B(g) + C(g)$, initially pressure of A is 1 bar.
Initially: total pressure = 1 bar.
After 100 min: total pressure = 1.5 bar.
Using initial and final pressures, if x bar of A decomposes, then A becomes (1-x), B = x, C = x.
Total pressure = 1-x+x+x = 1+x = 1.5, so x = 0.5 bar.
For a first-order reaction: $k = \frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[A]_0}{[A]}$.
Using pressures instead: $k = \frac{2.303}{100} \log \frac{1}{0.5}$
$=\frac{2.303}{100} \times \log 2 = \frac{2.303 \times 0.3}{100} = \frac{0.691}{100}$
$= 6.91 \times 10^{-3}$ min$^{-1}$ ≈ $6.9 \times 10^{-3}$ min$^{-1}$

q:
Sucrose hydrolyses in acid solution into glucose and fructose following first order rate law with a half-life of 3.33 h at 25°C. After 9 h, the fraction of sucrose remaining is f. The value of log101f is ________ ×10-2. (Rounded off to the nearest integer)

[Assume: In10=2.303, ln 2=0.693]
question:
Sucrose hydrolyses in acid solution into glucose and fructose following first order rate law with a half-life of 3.33 h at 25°C. After 9 h, the fraction of sucrose remaining is f. The value of log101f is ________ ×10-2. (Rounded off to the nearest integer)

[Assume: In10=2.303, ln 2=0.693]
solution:
C12H22O11+H2O→t1/2=103hrorderC6H12O6Glucose+C6H12O6Fructose

t=0  a=A0

t=9hr  a-x=At

from I order kinetic: k×t2.303=logA0At

⇒ln2×9103×2.303=log1f

⇒0.693×9×323.03=log1f

⇒log1f=0.81246=81.24×10-2

⇒x=81

q: During the nuclear explosion, one of the products is <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi></mrow><mrow><mn>90</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> with half life of <math><mn>6.93</mn></math> years. If <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi></mrow><mrow><mn>90</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> was absorbed in the bones of a newly born baby in place of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi></math> how much time, in years, is required to reduce it by <math><mn>90</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math> if it is not lost metabolically ____________.
question: During the nuclear explosion, one of the products is <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi></mrow><mrow><mn>90</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> with half life of <math><mn>6.93</mn></math> years. If <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi></mrow><mrow><mn>90</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> was absorbed in the bones of a newly born baby in place of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi></math> how much time, in years, is required to reduce it by <math><mn>90</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math> if it is not lost metabolically ____________.
solution:
Radioactive disintegration is always a first order reaction for a first order reaction. Radioactive decay is the process by which an unstable atomic nucleus loses energy by radiation.
 
Kt=2.303  log A o A t
 
where A o = initial concentration

A t = concentration at time ‘t’

t 1 2 = 0.693 K K= 0.693 t 1 2 = 0.693 6.93 ⇒0.1 yent s −1 t 90% = 2.303 0.1 log 100 10 t 90% =23.03 years.

subject: Chemistry
q:
During Kinetic study of reaction \(2 A+B \rightarrow C+D\), the following results were obtained :
\(\begin{array}{llll} 
& \text { A [M] } & \text { B [M] } & \text { initial rate of formation of D } \\
\text { I } & 0.1 & 0.1 & 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \\
\text { II } & 0.3 & 0.2 & 7.20 \times 10^{-2} \\
\text { III } & 0.3 & 0.4 & 2.88 \times 10^{-1} \\
\text { IV } & 0.4 & 0.1 & 2.40 \times 10^{-2} \\
\end{array}\)
Based on above data, overall order of the reaction is ______
question:
During Kinetic study of reaction \(2 A+B \rightarrow C+D\), the following results were obtained :
\(\begin{array}{llll} 
& \text { A [M] } & \text { B [M] } & \text { initial rate of formation of D } \\
\text { I } & 0.1 & 0.1 & 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \\
\text { II } & 0.3 & 0.2 & 7.20 \times 10^{-2} \\
\text { III } & 0.3 & 0.4 & 2.88 \times 10^{-1} \\
\text { IV } & 0.4 & 0.1 & 2.40 \times 10^{-2} \\
\end{array}\)
Based on above data, overall order of the reaction is ______
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{K}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{y}} \\ & (\mathrm{I}) 6 \times 10^{-3}=\mathrm{K}[0.1]^{\mathrm{x}}[0.1]^{\mathrm{y}} \\ & (\mathrm{IV}) 2.4 \times 10^{-2}=\mathrm{K}[0.4]^{\mathrm{x}}[0.1]^{\mathrm{y}} \\ & \quad(\mathrm{IV}) /(\mathrm{I}) \\ & 4=(4)^{\mathrm{x}} \\ & \quad \mathrm{x}=1 \\ & \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{K}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{y}}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { (III) } 2.88 \times 10^{-1}=\mathrm{K}[0.3]^{\mathrm{x}}[0.4]^{\mathrm{y}} \\ & \text { (II) } 7.2 \times 10^{-2}=\mathrm{K}[0.3]^{\mathrm{x}}[0.2]^{\mathrm{y}} \\ & \text { (III)/(II) }\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & 4=2^y \\ & y=2\end{aligned}$
Overall order $=x+y=1+2=3$

subject: Chemistry
q:
For the reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ the following graph was obtained. The time required (in seconds) for the concentration of A to reduce to $2.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ (if the initial concentration of A was $50 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$) is _______ (Nearest integer)
Given : $\log 2=0.3010$ [We can assume in a hypothetical situation that the graph is correct, assuming its first order reaction, in reality the question is wrong.]
question:
For the reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ the following graph was obtained. The time required (in seconds) for the concentration of A to reduce to $2.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ (if the initial concentration of A was $50 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$) is _______ (Nearest integer)
Given : $\log 2=0.3010$ [We can assume in a hypothetical situation that the graph is correct, assuming its first order reaction, in reality the question is wrong.]
solution:
As it is difficult to predict order using data provided in graph.
For specific time interval $0-5 \mathrm{sec}, 5-10 \mathrm{sec}$ and $10-15 \mathrm{sec}$. order comes to be zero, but graph is not a straight line.
Assuming $1^{\text {st }}$ order kinetics
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \ln \frac{\mathrm{~A}_0}{\mathrm{~A}_{\mathrm{t}}} \\
& \mathrm{~K}=\frac{1}{10} \ln \frac{40}{20}
\end{aligned}$
Time required to reduce to $2.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \ln \frac{50}{2.5} \\
& \frac{1}{10} \ln 2=\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \ln 20 \\
& \mathrm{t}=\frac{1.3010 \times 10}{0.3010}=43.3 \mathrm{sec}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The decomposition of formic acid on gold surface follows first order kinetics. If the rate constant at 300 K is 1.0×10-3 s-1 and the activation energy Ea=11.488 kJ mol-1, the rate constant at 200 K is _________ ×10-5 s-1. (Round of to the Nearest Integer).

(Given R=8.314 J mol-1 K-1)
question:
The decomposition of formic acid on gold surface follows first order kinetics. If the rate constant at 300 K is 1.0×10-3 s-1 and the activation energy Ea=11.488 kJ mol-1, the rate constant at 200 K is _________ ×10-5 s-1. (Round of to the Nearest Integer).

(Given R=8.314 J mol-1 K-1)
solution:
K300=10-3   K200=?

Ea=11.488KJ/mole  R=8.314 J/mole-K

so ℓnK300 K200=EaR1200-1300

ℓnK300 K200=11.488×1000×1008.314×200×300

=2.303

=ℓn10

so K300 K200=10

K200=110×K300=10-4

=10×10-5 sec-1
